review_text: The smell of butter is very strong you look great but the taste is very mediocre compared to the price
issue: The butter smell is overly strong and unpleasant
fixes: Deploy a real‑time VOC sensor (e.g., AirVisual Pro) on the butter storage rack and set an automated alert in your CMMS when readings exceed 50 ppb, prompting immediate refrigeration adjustment or batch quarantine. | - Install calibrated odor meters (such as Sensidyne Olfactometer) at packaging lines and require operators to record a daily “odor score” in the production log; any reading above 7 triggers a corrective action checklist. | - Implement a HACCP‑based SOP that includes a step‑by‑step temperature verification (target range: −18 °C to +5 °C) before each shift, documented via iAuditor mobile forms with photo evidence. | - Integrate a QR‑code linked to a short, interactive microlearning module in your LMS that walks staff through proper butter handling techniques, measuring completion rates and quiz scores monthly.
rationale: The recommendations are highly relevant and concrete, naming specific tools and processes. While actionability is strong, implementing VOC sensors and olfactometers isn't 'trivial' and requires calibration and potentially significant overhead, slightly lowering the feasibility score. HACCP SOPs are standard practice and the microlearning module is reasonable, but also represents an investment of time and resources.

review_text: The smell of butter is very strong you look great but the taste is very mediocre compared to the price
issue: The taste is mediocre and does not meet expectations
fixes: Deploy a calibrated sensory evaluation panel (e.g., ISO 8589) with trained tasters who record scores on a standardized 9‑point hedonic scale; require any recipe variant to achieve ≥8 average before it goes live. | - Integrate ChefTec’s ingredient‑level costing and nutrient analysis module to automatically flag any dish whose cost per calorie exceeds the target margin by more than 10 %, prompting immediate reformulation. | - Install IoT temperature probes linked to a Kitchen Display System that sends real‑time alerts when cooking temperatures deviate >±2 °C from preset values, ensuring consistent texture and flavor delivery. | - Create a weekly “Flavor Benchmark” dashboard in Power BI that tracks key flavor compounds measured via GC–MS, triggers a corrective‑action ticket when a compound falls below its target threshold, and logs the remedial adjustment for future refinement.

review_text: The smell of butter is very strong you look great but the taste is very mediocre compared to the price
issue: The product is overpriced relative to its quality
fixes: Conduct a competitive price‑benchmark study with tools like PriceIntelligence to identify pricing gaps and adjust SKU prices within ±10% of market averages. | - Deploy a cost‑to‑serve model in Excel linked to your ERP (e.g., SAP) to quantify material, labor, and overhead per unit; then set a target margin threshold (e.g., ≥25%) and trigger alerts when margins fall below it. | - Launch a tiered SKU strategy using a Power BI dashboard that segments products by perceived value and sets distinct price points, automatically updating pricing tags on the e‑commerce site via Shopify Scripts. | - Introduce a post‑purchase NPS survey integrated with Qualtrics that asks “Did you feel the product was priced fairly?” and routes any negative responses to a dedicated quality‑price remediation queue in Asana for immediate follow‑up.
rationale: The recommendations are highly relevant to the 'value for money' theme and include concrete tools. Actionability is strong, but assumes a user knows how to integrate some of these tools. Feasibility is impacted by the ERP integration and the setup of Shopify Scripts, both of which would require development work and are not trivial.

review_text: The strawberry cheesecake was 7/10 (good but not the best). The brownie was 5/10 (just average American pastry with too much sugar and not that much flavor). Coffees were disappointing, below average. The place was disappointing as the place right in front was amazing.
issue: Strawberry cheesecake was only average, not outstanding
fixes: Deploy a sensory evaluation panel (e.g., ISO 8589) twice per month using a calibrated scorecard to measure flavor intensity, texture, and overall satisfaction; record results in a shared Google Sheet and trigger corrective actions when any metric falls below the target range. | - Introduce a standardized recipe management workflow in ChefTec, version‑control all cheesecake formulations with a change‑request log, and require sign‑off by the head chef before each batch is produced. | - Install IoT temperature/humidity sensors on the pastry display case and link them to a real‑time dashboard (e.g., Power BI) that alerts the floor manager if conditions deviate ±2 °C from the optimal storage zone, prompting immediate re‑cooling or removal. | - Conduct a quarterly supplier audit using the Supplier Quality Scorecard in SAP Ariba, rating freshness, consistency, and defect rates, and automatically suspend non‑compliant vendors until performance improves.
rationale: The recommendations are very concrete with specific tools named, directly addressing quality concerns. While relevant, the initial sensory panel seems overkill for a single 'average' cheesecake complaint, and some solutions (like full SAP Ariba integration) may have hidden costs. Feasibility is slightly lowered as some recommendations require more than trivial effort, including potentially expensive software or supplier management changes.

review_text: The strawberry cheesecake was 7/10 (good but not the best). The brownie was 5/10 (just average American pastry with too much sugar and not that much flavor). Coffees were disappointing, below average. The place was disappointing as the place right in front was amazing.
issue: Brownie was overly sweet and lacked flavor
fixes: Install calibrated digital refractometers (e.g., BrixMaster) on production lines to measure sugar concentration in real time and set a target Brix range (≤ 70) that triggers automatic recipe adjustments via the bakery’s ERP (SAP PP). | - Introduce a sensory panel with a standardized 9‑point hedonic scoring sheet (using ISO 8586 guidelines) before packaging, requiring a minimum average score of 7.0 before the batch is released. | - Add a “Flavor Boost” step using a precise dosing pump (e.g., peristaltic pump) to add 10 g of high‑flavor cocoa extract per kilogram of batter, and log the addition in the MES (Rockwell FactoryTalk) for traceability. | - Deploy IoT temperature and humidity sensors (e.g., Sensitech TempTale) integrated into the oven control system to maintain optimal baking conditions (± 2 °C/± 5 % RH), reducing off‑flavors caused by over‑browning.
rationale: The recommendations directly address the brownie issue (sweetness and flavor) and are highly concrete with tool mentions. Actionability is strong, though frontline workers might need some training on ERP/MES integration. Feasibility is the only area for deduction; SAP PP, Rockwell FactoryTalk, and even ISO 8586 implementation carry significant effort and cost that isn't acknowledged.

issue: Overall place was disappointing compared to nearby location
fixes: Deploy a real‑time foot‑traffic heat map (e.g., using Google Analytics Location Reports) to identify peak hours and adjust staffing schedules weekly in the scheduling software (e.g., Deputy) to ensure adequate front‑line staff during those periods. | - Launch a post‑visit micro‑survey via Qualtrics triggered within 24 hours; automatically route any “disappointed” response to the operations manager’s Slack channel for immediate follow‑up and corrective action logging. | - Implement a quarterly mystery‑shopper program using Secret Shopper Pro, assigning a KPI score for ambiance, cleanliness, and service speed; tie 10 % of team bonuses to meeting or exceeding the average score threshold. | - Install digital signage powered by Medallia Experience Management to display live NPS scores and recent guest feedback, updating every shift change so employees can instantly see how they’re contributing to overall satisfaction.
rationale: The recommendations are generally relevant to improving service quality given the customer's complaint, though they focus heavily on measurement and less on addressing the root cause of *why* the location was disappointing. The concreteness is excellent with named tools and specific KPIs. Feasibility is moderate because integrating these solutions requires budget for tools like Qualtrics and Secret Shopper Pro, as well as training and ongoing management.

review_text: I went here whilst staying at The Venetian hotel over New Year's Eve and honestly had a terrible experience. I was very excited to visit the bakery as I used to watch cake boss on TV and absolutely LOVED the show. I tried the lobster tail which is highly overrated, the cupcakes which were dry and a few other pastries that aren't even worth mentioning as I don't even remember anymore they left no impression on me. Definitely does not deserve to have its own tv show- there are much better bakeries out there.
issue: Lobster tail was disappointing and not worth the hype
fixes: Deploy a daily sensory‑scoring sheet (e.g., a Google Sheet with “Flavor,” “Texture,” and “Overall” scores) and require the kitchen manager to sign off on each lobster batch before service, enforcing consistent quality checkpoints. | - Introduce a QR‑code‑linked post‑service survey (via SurveyMonkey) that asks diners to rate lobster texture on a 1‑10 scale; trigger an automatic alert in Slack when average score falls below 7, prompting immediate corrective action. | - Install a calibrated sous‑vide machine and set the exact cooking parameters (temperature = 45 °C, time = 15 min) for all lobster tails, recording cook times in a digital log that is reviewed weekly by the culinary director. | - Implement a weekly supplier audit using a standardized checklist in Asana that verifies shell condition, weight variance, and freshness date compliance, with any non‑conformance triggering a replacement order within 24 hours.
rationale: The recommendations are directly related to food quality and address the lobster tail issue. While actionable, some require training/explanation (kitchen manager sign-off, Slack alert configuration) preventing a perfect score. The concreteness is excellent, naming specific tools. Feasibility is high, but weekly audits/director review add some overhead, costing time and potentially resources.

issue: Cupcakes were dry
fixes: Standardize moisture testing:** Install a handheld moisture meter (e.g., Extech MO55) on every shift and require bakers to record the target range (e.g., 18‑20 % moisture) in the production log before cupcakes are released to the line. | - **Adjust bake time by real‑time temperature monitoring:** Retrofit ovens with IoT‑enabled thermocouple sensors linked to a cloud dashboard (such as Sensaphone) that alerts when oven temperature deviates >2 °C from the preset profile, ensuring consistent baking conditions. | - **Implement a calibrated oven calibration routine:** Use a digital infrared thermometer (e.g., Fluke 62 Max) after every batch to verify oven temperature accuracy; any variance outside ±1 °C triggers a corrective maintenance ticket in the CMMS (e.g., Fiix). | - **Add a pre‑bake hydration step:** Spray cupcakes with a 0.5 % water solution using a handheld spray bottle and measure the soak time with a stopwatch, documenting the exact application rate in the recipe SOP. | - **Track ingredient freshness with barcode scanning:** Integrate the bakery’s ERP (e.g., SAP Business One) with a handheld scanner (e.g., Zebra DS2208) to capture lot numbers for flour, butter, and eggs; set automatic expiration alerts when ingredients exceed their shelf life by more than 7 days.
rationale: The recommendations are highly relevant and concrete, naming specific tools and providing measurable actions. However, 'Adjust bake time' and 'Track ingredient freshness' require cross-department coordination (IT for IoT/ERP integration) and ongoing maintenance of the systems, lowering the feasibility score. Actionability is strong, but bakers/staff would need training on *why* these metrics matter, lowering it from a 5 to a 4.

review_text: Well I didn't go but I am sure the food is good. I was not going to wait over an hour in line to be rushed through. If I had known I would not have wasted my time going. Very disappointed.
issue: Long wait time of over an hour in line
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based queue management system (e.g., QLess) integrated with existing POS to display real‑time wait times on digital screens and automatically send SMS alerts when guests reach a preset threshold (e.g., 15 minutes), reducing perceived wait time by at least 20 %. | - Install self‑service kiosks (e.g., Toast Kiosk) for routine transactions such as menu selection, order placement, and payment, targeting a 30 % reduction in average queue length during peak hours. | - Introduce a predictive staffing model in the workforce‑management software (e.g., Kronos) that uses historical foot‑traffic data to schedule additional staff 10 minutes before predicted surge points, ensuring a minimum of one extra employee per 50 customers during high‑volume periods. | - Implement a mobile ordering app (e.g., Toast Takeout) linked to the kitchen display system so orders are pre‑prepared while customers wait, cutting physical line time by up to 40 % during lunch rushes.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the long wait time issue, and each is incredibly concrete with tool examples and even suggested metrics. Actionability is strong, but implementing these *fully* isn't trivial and will require work beyond simple configuration, lowering the Actionability and Feasibility slightly. The feasibility assumes existing POS/WFM/KDS integrations which is not explicitly stated.

review_text: Well I didn't go but I am sure the food is good. I was not going to wait over an hour in line to be rushed through. If I had known I would not have wasted my time going. Very disappointed.
issue: Being rushed through the service
fixes: Deploy a “Wait Time” dashboard in Zendesk Explore that flags any ticket where the agent’s average handling time exceeds 5 minutes, prompting supervisors to intervene immediately via Slack alerts. | - Introduce a mandatory 60‑second pause after every interaction (e.g., by setting a timer in Microsoft Teams) and log the pause duration in a shared Google Sheet to track compliance weekly. | - Implement a real‑time sentiment monitoring script in Talkdesk that triggers a supervisor alert when a call receives a negative rating on pace, enabling instant coaching before the next call. | - Create a scripted empathy prompt (“I see you’re in a hurry; let me take a moment to understand your priority”) and embed it into the CRM workflow so agents see it automatically before each reply, measured by monthly audit reports. | - Schedule a bi‑weekly 15‑minute “Rush‑Free Service” huddle using the built‑in video conferencing feature in Zoom, where agents review the top three flagged tickets from the Zendesk dashboard and agree on corrective actions.
rationale: The recommendations directly address the rushing issue. Actionability is high, though some (like the bi-weekly huddle) feel less critical than others. Feasibility suffers because the sentiment monitoring script and supervisor intervention require development/integration work; weekly Google Sheet logging also seems tedious and will likely have compliance issues. Overall, a strong set of recommendations, but not 'trivial' to implement.

review_text: I was so excited to go here, but that excitement soon turned to disappointment. We had never been to a Carlo's Bakery in person, and we are fans of Buddy, of course, so we had to go while in Vegas. We waited in a decently long line, which gave us time to look at all the things that were wrong with this place. The display cases were filthy and messy. Frosting, sprinkles and crumbs were everywhere. There were even some cupcakes that had fallen upside down that just laid there in the case looking oh so sad. Several of the trays were empty, one of those being cannolis. One of the main reasons we wanted to go to Carlo's was was to try one of their famous cannolis. Sadly, that didn't happen. Mind you this was in the middle of the day. It wasn't like we were there 10 minutes til close. On top of that the food that was in the case was not properly labeled. Many things had no labels at all. This can be dangerous for folks with food allergies and annoying to other people trying to communicate what they want to the workers. We ended up getting a lobster tail and a napoleon type thing (a layered cake. no sign, so who knows what it was!). The lobster tails tasted stale and like it had sat in a fridge for a week. The cake thing was just not good. The flavor was not good. I took one bite of each and wished I had spent that $20 on a slot machine instead of desserts that wal-mart can do better than. I really hope Buddy and his family get a handle on things and get their locations back in shape, because I don't think this is the quality they had in mind for their customers.
issue: Display cases were filthy and messy with frosting, sprinkles, crumbs, and fallen cupcakes
fixes: Install IoT‑enabled smart sensors (e.g., Senseware dust detectors) on display case doors that trigger an automated cleaning ticket in the facilities management software when particulate levels exceed 150 µg/m³. | - Deploy a digital checklist app such as iAuditor with a “Case Cleanliness” module that staff complete before opening; any item marked incomplete generates a real‑time alert to the floor manager via Slack. | - Schedule nightly deep‑cleaning runs using a calibrated microfiber cloth and food‑grade sanitizing spray (e.g., EPA‑registered Clorox Total 360), logged in a daily KPI dashboard tracked by Power BI. | - Set up a rotating “display‑case rotation” schedule in the workforce management tool Kronos, assigning one employee per shift to wipe down surfaces, ensuring coverage every two hours during peak service periods.
rationale: The recommendations are highly relevant and concrete, naming specific tools. Actionability is good, though frontline workers might need some training on the IoT sensors and Power BI. Feasibility is slightly lower because IoT sensors and Power BI integration represent a moderate effort and cost, even if manageable.

fixes: Deploy a kitchen display system (KDS) such as Toast POS with real‑time stock alerts that automatically flag any item tray that falls below its minimum count and triggers an immediate reorder. | - Integrate a barcode‑based inventory management module into the ordering workflow so staff scan each tray’s QR code before service; if the scan fails, the system blocks the order until the tray is restocked. | - Implement a daily “Full Tray Checklist” in Asana where the line lead verifies all menu items are present on each station and records any exceptions, with a KPI target of zero missed items per shift. | - Set up a weekly demand forecast in Power BI that pulls sales data from Stripe and allocates minimum batch sizes to each tray type, updating production schedules via Microsoft Project to ensure consistent supply levels.
rationale: The recommendations directly address the product availability issue and use concrete tools. Actionability is strong, but a frontline worker may need some training on integrating barcode scanning into the ordering workflow. fixes: Deploy a mobile barcode‑labeling app (e.g., Zebra MotionWorks) integrated with the POS so every item scanned at checkout automatically generates a QR code label printed on demand, ensuring all food is labeled before leaving the kitchen. | - Create a daily “Label Compliance” checklist in Asana that requires staff to scan a random sample of products and upload proof images to the task board; any missing labels trigger an automatic alert to the shift supervisor. | - Install RFID‑enabled shelf sensors linked to the inventory management system (e.g., SAP EWM) that flag any SKU without a matching tag and schedule an immediate corrective work order in ServiceNow. | - Launch a weekly 15‑minute audit using iAuditor’s pre‑built “Label Accuracy” template, assigning scores based on percentage of labeled vs. unlabeled items; results are fed into a Power BI dashboard with a target ≥ 98% compliance. | - Introduce a vendor scorecard in Coupa Procurement that ties a 0.25 % penalty to any supplier whose shipments arrive without required allergen labels, incentivizing suppliers to comply and enabling traceability from receipt to display.
rationale: The recommendations are highly relevant and concrete, naming specific tools for each. Actionability is strong, though some frontline workers might need training on integrating systems. Feasibility is where points are deducted; RFID sensors and SAP EWM are expensive and complex, and vendor scorecards require significant procurement setup.

rationale: The recommendations directly address food quality issues and are specific with tool names and metrics. Actionability is high, but implementation might require training or some initial configuration. Feasibility is a concern due to the IoT devices and HVAC integration which is not 'trivial'.

review_text: I was so excited to go here, but that excitement soon turned to disappointment. We had never been to a Carlo's Bakery in person, and we are fans of Buddy, of course, so we had to go while in Vegas. We waited in a decently long line, which gave us time to look at all the things that were wrong with this place. The display cases were filthy and messy. Frosting, sprinkles and crumbs were everywhere. There were even some cupcakes that had fallen upside down that just laid there in the case looking oh so sad. Several of the trays were empty, one of those being cannolis. One of the main reasons we wanted to go to Carlo's was was to try one of their famous cannolis. Sadly, that didn't happen. Mind you this was in the middle of the day. It wasn't like we were there 10 minutes til close. On top of that the food that was in the case was not properly labeled. Many things had no labels at all. This can be dangerous for folks with food allergies and annoying to other people trying to communicate what they want to the workers. We ended up getting a lobster tail and a napoleon type thing (a layered cake. no sign, so who knows what it was!). The lobster tails tasted stale and like it had sat in a fridge for a week. The cake thing was just not good. The flavor was not good. I took one bite of each and wished I had spent that $20 on a slot machine instead of desserts that wal-mart can do better than. I really hope Buddy and his family get a handle on things and get their locations back in shape, because I don't think this is the quality they had in mind for their customers.
issue: Customers experienced a long wait in line before being served
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based virtual queue (e.g., QLess) that sends customers a SMS ticket number upon arrival, displays real‑time wait estimates on digital signage, and automatically releases tickets when staff are ready. | - Introduce self‑service kiosks integrated with your POS system (using Square Kiosk) so customers can place orders without joining the physical line, reducing the “foot traffic” load by up to 30 %. | - Implement a demand‑forecasting model in Power BI that pulls hourly footfall data from security cameras and adjusts staffing schedules via the workforce management tool Kronos, targeting a maximum average wait time of <2 minutes during peak periods. | - Launch a mobile pre‑order app (built with React Native) that lets customers select items and pick‑up times ahead of store entry, cutting in‑store queue length by at least 15 % within two weeks.
rationale: The recommendations directly address the wait time issue and propose concrete solutions. Actionability is strong, with specific tools named; however, implementation isn't *trivial*. Feasibility is lowered due to the integration/development effort implied in a custom app, Power BI model, and potential POS/kiosk integration complexity, requiring moderate budget and coordination.

review_text: Very disappointing, got the fabled Cannoli's and what an absolute waste of money. I had actually gotten some from a different bakery at Paris and assumed that as well as those tasted, Carlos's would be better, boy was I wrong! Buddy needs to stick to making cakes that look good, and I'm sure don't taste good.
issue: The cannoli were disappointing and tasted bad, not meeting expectations
fixes: Deploy a weekly sensory‑evaluation panel using ISO 8589 standards (sensory wheel) on all batches of cannoli; record scores in a Google Sheet and trigger a “rework” flag when average taste rating falls below 8/10. | - Install temperature‑logging sensors (e.g., ThermoWorks ChefAlarm) on the filling and baking stations, set alerts at ±2 °C from target ranges, and review logs daily to ensure consistent product conditions. | - Introduce a HACCP‑based recipe SOP with digital checklists (e.g., iAuditor) that requires staff to verify ingredient freshness, mixing time, and bake temperature before each batch is released. | - Conduct quarterly supplier audits using a Scorecard template (Excel) that rates raw material quality (moisture content, shelf life, flavor intensity) against defined benchmarks, and adjust vendor contracts based on audit results. | - Implement a real‑time customer feedback loop via QR‑code surveys linked to Qualtrics, collecting “taste satisfaction” scores after purchase; automatically generate a corrective‑action ticket in Jira if the NPS drops below 70 within 24 hours.
rationale: The recommendations directly address the cannoli quality issue and offer specific, measurable actions. However, some suggestions (supplier audits, quarterly reviews) are not trivial to implement and require more effort than 'easy' rollout, and the Jira ticket trigger threshold seems arbitrary without baseline data. Despite this, the high degree of specificity earns a good score.

issue: The product was considered a waste of money, indicating poor value for money
fixes: Deploy a post‑purchase NPS survey via Qualtrics that includes a “Value Score” question (0‑10); automatically flag any rating ≤ 6 and trigger a follow‑up email within 48 hours offering a partial refund or upgrade. | - Implement a tiered pricing matrix in Salesforce CPQ that clearly maps feature bundles to price points, then run weekly profit‑margin analytics on the built‑in forecasting model to adjust bundle prices based on margin thresholds. | - Launch a “Value Assurance Program” where customers receive a 15% discount coupon after completing a short 5‑question online checklist (e.g., “Did you achieve your main outcome?”) within 30 days, measured by redemption rates tracked in HubSpot. | - Create a monthly Value‑ROI dashboard in Power BI that pulls usage data from Mixpanel and cost metrics from Google Analytics; set KPIs to increase average ROI per user by at least 10% quarter over quarter and review results with the product team during sprint retrospectives.
rationale: The recommendations largely address the 'Value' theme directly and utilize concrete tools. Points were deducted from 'feasibility' because implementing Salesforce CPQ changes and Power BI dashboards require significant effort and cross-departmental coordination; the 'Value Assurance Program' also involves a discount program which has cost implications. While actionable, the 'weekly profit-margin analytics' piece could be overly burdensome without clear justification.

review_text: Awful experience. Guest specialist Alyssa had a horrible attitude. She should be fired. The cashier Akberet, however, was very polite. Thumbs down for bad service!
issue: Guest specialist displayed a horrible attitude, leading to a bad service experience
fixes: Deploy a real‑time sentiment monitoring dashboard (e.g., Medallia Experience) on guest specialist desktops that flags any negative sentiment score < 60 within 30 seconds, automatically routing the interaction to a senior manager for immediate coaching. | - Introduce a mandatory “Attitude & Etiquette” micro‑learning module in Lessonly, requiring all specialists to complete a 10‑minute scenario‑based quiz with a minimum 90% pass rate before each shift starts. | - Launch a quarterly “Voice‑of‑the‑Customer” pulse survey via SurveyMonkey, targeting 100 randomly selected guests; set a target KPI that ≥95% of respondents rate the specialist’s demeanor as “professional,” and trigger corrective action if the threshold is missed. | - Implement a peer‑review program using a shared Google Sheet where colleagues log any observed unprofessional behavior, prompting an automatic Slack notification to the supervisor who must schedule a remediation session within 48 hours.
rationale: The recommendations are relevant and concrete, naming specific tools. Actionability is good, though the 'corrective action' trigger in the survey recommendation is vague. The peer-review program using Google Sheets feels a bit clunky and introduces overhead, decreasing feasibility, and the shift-start quiz may disrupt workflow.

review_text: Place was horrible. First and foremost, we grabbed a number which was B89 and waited over 2 hours for our number to get called. One of the most disorganized companies I have ever seen. I understand things were busy but the way of doing business was horrible and we noticed a lot of people leaving purely because of the wait. Second, once we finally got our number called, I was informed the pie that I had ordered 4 hours earlier was not made. The manager came out and spoke to me and before even apologizing, blamed it on me because I had not pre-paid. She tried to explain how the process worked if I would have pre-paid for the pie but did not apologize or offer a solution outside of waiting another 40 minutes for my pie to be made. Absolute worst customer service I have ever seen from a "manager." On top of that, pre-paying for my pie was never even provided to me as an option which only made things worse. Would not recommend this to anyone unless you are looking to waste a few hours on the strip and if you don't want to get your order correct.
issue: Excessive wait time of over two hours before being called
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based queue management platform (e.g., NICE inContact) with real‑time SLA alerts that trigger automatic escalation when a call exceeds 20 minutes, routing it instantly to a senior agent or overflow pool. | - Integrate a predictive‑routing engine such as Genesys Cloud that analyzes caller sentiment scores from previous interactions and routes high‑priority callers to the next available agent within 60 seconds. | - Implement a workforce‑management module (e.g., Verint WFM) to forecast peak call volumes and auto‑schedule additional agents during identified “hot spots,” reducing average handle time by at least 15 %. | - Launch a self‑service IVR portal using Twilio Flex that offers pre‑qualified FAQs and instant callback requests, targeting a 30 % reduction in total inbound volume while ensuring callbacks are issued within 10 minutes.
rationale: The recommendations are largely relevant to the wait time issue, and the use of specific tools boosts concreteness. However, feasibility is low; implementing these platforms is not trivial and would require significant budget and integration efforts. Actionability is moderate - while tools are named, *how* to integrate and configure them is not explained, and specific escalation criteria or IVR content aren't detailed.

issue: Disorganized operations causing long waits and customers leaving
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based queue management system (e.g., QLess) integrated with your POS so customers receive SMS appointment times and wait alerts, reducing on‑site queues by at least 20 % within two weeks. | - Implement a real‑time workforce‑management dashboard in Deputy that pulls live sales data from Shopify and automatically schedules extra staff when forecasted demand exceeds capacity thresholds, ensuring coverage during peak periods. | - Launch a daily “Operations Huddle” where floor supervisors log any operational disruptions in a shared Google Sheet; set a KPI of resolving ≥90 % of issues within 15 minutes and track progress via weekly Power BI reports. | - Create a centralized order‑fulfillment workflow in Asana with automated SLAs (e.g., “pick completed within 10 min”) and assign owners per task, then monitor completion rates through the built‑in analytics to achieve ≤2 % order‑related abandonments.
rationale: The recommendations are directly related to the theme of service quality and address the issue of disorganized operations. They are mostly actionable, although the Deputy suggestion relies on some existing Shopify integration. Concreteness is strong with specific tool mentions and KPIs. Feasibility is lowered because implementing new systems (QLess, Deputy, Asana) and even a shared Google Sheet with reporting requires effort and training beyond 'trivial'.

issue: Ordered pie not prepared despite being placed hours earlier
fixes: Deploy a kitchen display system (e.g., Toast KDS) integrated with the order‑management platform so every ticket is automatically flagged “ready” once prep time exceeds the preset threshold (e.g., > 20 minutes). | - Introduce a real‑time order status dashboard in the staff mobile app (e.g., HotSchedules) that displays “prepped,” “in‑prep,” and “out‑of‑stock” tags for each item, enabling immediate supervisor alerts when a pie remains “in‑prep” after its scheduled window. | - Implement a mandatory “Order Complete” checklist step in the POS workflow that requires the prep line manager to digitally sign off on each ticket before it can be marked “ready.” The sign‑off triggers an automated Slack notification to the floor team. | - Set up a daily Power BI KPI report that tracks average “order‑to‑service” time per SKU; any item exceeding the target by more than 15 minutes generates an email alert to the shift lead for corrective action.

The feasibility score is lowered because some integrations (Talkdesk AI) and surveys require budget/ongoing effort.

issue: No pre‑payment option provided, leading to confusion about ordering process
fixes: Deploy Stripe’s “Pay Later” button (or PayPal’s “Buy Now Pay Later”) on all product pages and checkout flows via your existing e‑commerce platform’s integration layer, so customers see a clear pre‑payment choice before submitting their order. | - Add a static FAQ banner to the cart page in Shopify that links to a dedicated “Pre‑Payment Options” article hosted on Zendesk Guide, ensuring customers always have a clickable reference point when they’re unsure about payment timing. | - Introduce an automatic email trigger from SendGrid that sends a confirmation message within 15 minutes of any order with the subject line “Order Confirmed – Pre‑Payment Required,” including a link to the pre‑payment portal. | - Schedule weekly A/B tests in Optimizely targeting the checkout flow to measure conversion lift from adding a “Pre‑Payment” toggle versus the default “Immediate Payment” option, aiming for at least a 10% increase in orders with pre‑payment enabled.

review_text: Don't send your child in alone. The girl behind the counter told our grandson the machine was broken so his kid cup was filled only one-third of the way (about 5 tablespoons) and watery, although he still paid full price and tipped. When he returned to the car he was obviously disappointed so I went in to ask about it. The shift manager told me that's how they come now and then added, "Have a nice day." I guess rudeness and minion size portions is how they roll! As for us, we walked over to Culliver's where my grandson got a real size cup of ice cream with great customer service!
issue: Customer received a significantly smaller, watery portion of ice cream but was charged full price.
fixes: Deploy a tablet‑based portion‑control app (e.g., PortionPro+) at all serving stations that captures exact scoop weights; any scoop deviating >5 % from the standard triggers an automatic alert to the shift manager. | - Integrate POS data with a real‑time inventory management system such as MarketMan to flag orders where the itemized weight does not match the pre‑set portion size, preventing overcharging. | - Implement a weekly “Portion Accuracy Audit” using a digital checklist in iAuditor, assigning random servers to measure three scoops per shift and logging results against a KPI dashboard. | - Create a transparent pricing policy in the menu that lists portion volume and price, then display it on digital signage and require staff to verbally confirm the correct charge before each transaction.
rationale: The recommendations directly address the food quality issue (portion size and pricing) making them relevant.  Actionability is high, although implementing the POS integration might require some initial setup documentation. Feasibility is slightly lower because of the costs and coordination needed to implement new apps, systems, and audits – it's not trivial.

issue: Staff, including the manager, responded rudely and dismissively to the complaint.
fixes: Deploy a real‑time sentiment monitoring dashboard in Zendesk (or another ticketing platform) that flags any comment rated ≤ 3 stars by a customer and automatically routes it to a “Complaint Escalation” queue within 5 minutes. | - Introduce mandatory quarterly 90‑minute role‑play workshops on de‑escalation and empathy, recorded via Loom and reviewed with individual performance scores tied to bonus eligibility. | - Install QR‑code‑linked anonymous feedback stations at checkout points; aggregate results weekly into a Power BI report showing “rude response” incidents, triggering corrective coaching for staff members who exceed the defined threshold. | - Create a “First Response SLA” policy that requires managers to acknowledge complaints within 15 minutes and assign a resolution owner within 24 hours, tracked through the internal CRM’s “Case Status” field.
rationale: All recommendations directly address rudeness and dismissive behavior. Actionability is high, though some steps assume existing platform configurations. Feasibility is slightly reduced due to potential cost/effort of sentiment monitoring and the workload of quarterly workshops; also, relying on anonymous QR codes for incident tracking feels weak.
